summary: Southern Gothic author known for Catholic-influenced short stories and two novels, associated with grotesque characters experiencing moments of grace. Raised peacocks on her estate Andalusia in Milledgeville, Georgia.

works.name: A Good Man Is Hard to Find
works.description: O'Connor's most famous short story and the title story of her first collection. A family road trip goes horribly wrong when the Grandmother's mistaken memory of a house's location leads them off the main road. After a car crash caused by the cat Pitty Sing, the family encounters the escaped convict known as The Misfit. The Grandmother's final gesture of grace — touching the Misfit and saying 'you're one of my own children' — is met with three gunshots. The story also features a stop at Red Sammy Butts's restaurant, where the family eats in Tennessee, and the children John Wesley and June Star.

clues.clue: The Grandmother tells The Misfit 'you're one of my babies / my own children' before he shoots her
examples: a woman says 'Why you're one of my babies. You're one of my own children!' before touching an escaped convict who shoots her three times

works.name: Everything That Rises Must Converge
works.description: The title story of O'Connor's second collection, exploring racial tensions in the segregated South. Julian accompanies his racist mother on a bus ride to her YMCA reducing classes. His mother wears a distinctive purple hat and proudly mentions her grandfather owned 200 slaves. When a Black woman boards wearing the same hat, the mother condescendingly tries to give the woman's child a penny, prompting the woman to strike her with her purse. Julian's mother collapses from a stroke and dies, devastating Julian despite his pretense of moral superiority.

clues.clue: Julian's mother wears the same purple hat as a Black woman on the bus

works.name: Good Country People
works.description: A story about the philosophy PhD Joy Hopewell, who changed her name to Hulga to spite her mother. She has an artificial wooden leg, about which she is extremely sensitive. A traveling Bible salesman named Manley Pointer seduces her in a barn loft, convinces her to show him where her wooden leg joins on, then steals it. His hollowed-out Bible contains whiskey and pornographic cards, revealing that he is far from the 'good country people' her mother admires.

clues.clue: Bible salesman Manley Pointer steals Hulga's artificial/wooden leg

works.name: Wise Blood
works.description: O'Connor's first novel, set in Taulkinham, Tennessee. The veteran Hazel Motes founds the 'Church Without Christ,' where 'the blind don't see and the lame don't walk.' He encounters the street preacher Asa Hawks, who pretends to be blind (having faked blinding himself with quicklime), and Hawks's nymphomaniac daughter Sabbath Lily. Enoch Emery steals a shriveled mummy from a museum to serve as the 'new Jesus' and later murders the actor playing Gonga the Gorilla and steals his suit. Hazel blinds himself with quicklime and practices self-mortification — walking with stones and glass in his shoes and barbed wire around his chest — until his landlady discovers this.

works.name: The Violent Bear It Away
works.description: O'Connor's second novel about the teenager Francis Marion Tarwater (named after the Swamp Fox), who is kidnapped and raised by his great-uncle Mason to become a prophet. Mason wants Tarwater to baptize the mentally disabled boy Bishop, son of the atheist schoolteacher Rayber. When Mason dies, Tarwater gets drunk instead of burying him and flees to the city. On a fishing trip, Tarwater simultaneously drowns and baptizes Bishop. The novel ends with Tarwater being raped and abandoned in a forest. Rayber depends on a hearing aid, which old Tarwater once caused by shooting him.

clues.clue: Francis Marion Tarwater simultaneously drowns and baptizes the mentally disabled boy Bishop

works.name: Revelation
works.description: Set in a doctor's waiting room, Ruby Turpin is called a 'wart hog from hell' by the college student Mary Grace, who throws a book at her. This provocation leads Mrs. Turpin to have a vision of a procession to heaven — a bridge of living fire carrying 'white trash,' Black people, and respectable folk in a great reversal of social hierarchy. The story challenges Turpin's rigid sense of moral and social order.

works.name: The Life You Save May Be Your Own
works.description: The one-armed drifter Tom Shiftlet arrives at the Crater farm, marries the deaf daughter Lucynell, and abandons her at a roadside restaurant (the Hot Spot) during their honeymoon. He drives away with the car, seeing a turnip-shaped cloud and road signs reading 'Drive carefully: the life you save may be your own.' The story features a boy calling Shiftlet's mother a 'stinking pole cat' instead of an 'angel of Gawd.'

clues.clue: Tom Shiftlet abandons deaf Lucynell Crater at a diner/restaurant on their honeymoon

works.name: Greenleaf
works.description: Mrs. May is gored and killed by a bull that belongs to her tenant, the title farmhand Greenleaf. Before this, she cuts morbid stories from newspapers, buries them, and shouts 'Jesus stab me in the heart' — a prayer that is answered literally when the bull pierces her chest. The story appears in the collection Everything That Rises Must Converge.

clues.clue: Mrs. May is gored/killed by a bull
examples: Mrs. May being gored by a bull
an unruly bull belonging to her title farmhand gores her right in the chest

clues.clue: Mrs. May shouts 'Jesus stab me in the heart' before being literally gored
examples: cuts the morbid stories out of newspapers and buries them before shouting 'Jesus stab me in the heart'

works.name: The Lame Shall Enter First
works.description: The atheist social worker Sheppard devotes his attention to reforming the clubfooted juvenile delinquent Rufus Johnson, neglecting his own son Norton. Rufus convinces Norton that he can see his dead mother through a telescope if he dies before being corrupted, leading Norton to hang himself. The story explores how misguided charity can have devastating consequences.

clues.clue: Rufus tells Norton he can see his dead mother in heaven, leading Norton to hang himself

clues.clue: Sheppard attempts to reform the clubfooted Rufus Johnson
examples: the atheist Sheppard attempts to reform the clubfooted juvenile delinquent Rufus Johnson

works.name: Parker's Back
works.description: O. E. Parker (Obadiah Elihue) covers his body in tattoos, except for his back. After a tractor accident, he is inspired to get a 'flat stern Byzantine' tattoo of Christ on his back. His wife Sarah Ruth, who finds this idolatrous, beats him with a broom. Peacocks appear symbolically in the story, consistent with O'Connor's personal love of the birds.

clues.clue: Parker gets a tattoo of Christ on his back; his wife Sarah Ruth beats him with a broom for idolatry

clues.clue: Parker's full name is Obadiah Elihue; he goes by O. E.

works.name: A View of the Woods
works.description: A story about a grandfather and granddaughter in conflict over the sale of land. The girl, who declares herself 'pure Pitts' (not a Fortune like her grandfather), resists his plan to sell the pasture where her father grazes calves. The grandfather bashes her head against a rock and then suffers a fatal heart attack.

clues.clue: The girl declares she is 'pure Pitts'; the grandfather bashes her head against a rock, then has a heart attack
examples: she defiantly declares that she's 'pure PITTS' after beating her grandfather, leading him to fatally bash her head against a rock

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: O'Connor is identified as a Southern Gothic author, extremely Catholic, who lived with over 40 peacocks on her estate Andalusia in Milledgeville, Georgia. Her essay 'The King of the Birds' describes her passion for ranching peacocks. She reworked her Iowa Writers' Workshop thesis story 'The Geranium' into 'Judgment Day.' Her essay collection Mystery and Manners contains 'The Fiction Writer and His Country' and lectures on her work. She is sometimes called by her full name Mary Flannery O'Connor. Critics grapple with the racism in her work alongside its Catholic themes of grace.

works.name: Other Stories
works.description: Several other stories appear in the clues with lesser frequency. 'The Enduring Chill' features a writer who returns home thinking he is dying but only has undulant fever from drinking unpasteurized milk; he is disappointed when a Jesuit priest doesn't recognize James Joyce. 'The Comforts of Home' features Thomas who plants a gun in Sarah Ham's purse and accidentally shoots his mother. 'The Displaced Person' opens with a peacock following Mrs. Shortley; Mr. Guizac is killed by Shortley's tractor. 'The Partridge Festival' features a mass shooter. 'The Five-Forty-Eight' [misattributed in some clues] and other minor stories round out the collection.

clues.clue: The Partridge Festival: mass shooter at Azalea Festival

comprehensive_summary:
Flannery O'Connor is one of the most frequently clued American short story writers in quizbowl, identified consistently as a Southern Gothic author whose Catholic faith profoundly shaped her fiction. She lived on her estate Andalusia in Milledgeville, Georgia, where she famously kept over 40 peacocks, a passion documented in her essay 'The King of the Birds.' Her full name was Mary Flannery O'Connor.

Her most iconic story, 'A Good Man Is Hard to Find,' is clued with extraordinary frequency. The key moments include the family's stop at Red Sammy Butts's restaurant, the car crash caused by the cat Pitty Sing, and the climactic encounter with The Misfit, who kills the entire family after the Grandmother touches him and says 'you're one of my babies, you're one of my own children.' This moment of grace is central to O'Connor's theological vision.

'Everything That Rises Must Converge' is nearly as prominent, centering on Julian and his racist mother's bus ride to the YMCA. The mother's purple hat — identical to one worn by a Black woman — and her condescending offer of a penny to a Black child precipitate her fatal stroke. The story explores racial tension and moral blindness in the segregated South.

'Good Country People' features the philosophy PhD Hulga (born Joy) and the Bible salesman Manley Pointer, whose hollowed-out Bible contains whiskey and pornographic cards. He seduces Hulga in a barn loft and steals her wooden leg, a devastating act of violation.

O'Connor wrote two novels. Wise Blood follows the veteran Hazel Motes in Taulkinham as he founds the 'Church Without Christ.' Key details include Enoch Emery stealing a mummy for the 'new Jesus,' killing the Gonga the Gorilla actor, Asa Hawks faking blindness, and Motes's eventual self-blinding. The Violent Bear It Away concerns Francis Marion Tarwater, raised by his great-uncle Mason to be a prophet. The novel's central act is Tarwater's simultaneous drowning and baptism of the mentally disabled boy Bishop.

Other frequently clued stories include 'Revelation,' where Mary Grace calls Mrs. Turpin a 'wart hog from hell' in a waiting room, prompting a vision of a heavenly procession; 'The Life You Save May Be Your Own,' where one-armed Tom Shiftlet abandons deaf Lucynell Crater at a restaurant; 'Greenleaf,' where Mrs. May is gored by a bull after crying 'Jesus stab me in the heart'; 'The Lame Shall Enter First,' where Sheppard's neglect drives Norton to suicide; and 'Parker's Back,' where O. E. Parker's tattoo of Christ earns him a beating from his iconoclastic wife Sarah Ruth.

O'Connor's critical and personal essays also appear in clues. She reworked her Iowa Writers' Workshop thesis 'The Geranium' into 'Judgment Day.' Her essay collection Mystery and Manners contains reflections on her craft, including her comparison of herself to Dr. Johnson's blind housekeeper. Contemporary critics grapple with racism in her work alongside its Catholic themes of grace and redemption.
